title: Superficial Fungal Infections
authors: Joseph Pechacek, Carol A. Kauffman, Michail S. Lionakis

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Malassezia Infections
items.content: Malassezia infections, including tinea versicolor, folliculitis, and seborrheic dermatitis, are diagnosed clinically. Microscopic examination with potassium hydroxide reveals budding yeasts and hyphae. Disseminated infection requires culture in olive oil medium.
keyPoints: Diagnosed clinically; use KOH preparation for microscopy.
Culture requires sterile olive oil for disseminated infections.
Topical treatments: selenium sulfide, ketoconazole, terbinafine (2 weeks).
Oral itraconazole or fluconazole (200 mg/day) for 5–7 days in extensive cases.
Fungemia treated with amphotericin B or azoles like voriconazole; remove catheters and discontinue lipid infusions.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · Superficial Fungal Infections | MALASSEZIA INFECTIONS

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Epidemiology of Malassezia Infections
items.content: Malassezia species cause tinea versicolor, folliculitis, and seborrheic dermatitis. Tinea versicolor presents as hypo/hyperpigmented patches; folliculitis mimics bacterial forms; severe seborrheic dermatitis occurs in AIDS patients.
keyPoints: Tinea versicolor: flat round scaly patches on neck/chest/upper arms, can be pruritic.
Folliculitis: resembles bacterial, occurs on back/chest.
Seborrheic dermatitis: erythematous pruritic lesions in scalp/nasolabial folds; severe in AIDS.
Rare fungemia and disseminated infection mostly in premature neonates with parenteral lipid nutrition.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · Superficial Fungal Infections | MALASSEZIA INFECTIONS

items.title: Candida auris: An Emerging Multidrug-Resistant Threat
items.content: C. auris is a significant concern due to its multidrug resistance and ability to cause nosocomial outbreaks by colonizing human skin, healthcare environments, and medical devices.
keyPoints: C. auris grows avidly in human sweat and colonizes deep skin layers including hair follicles.
It has a propensity for long-term colonization of the human skin and is associated with multidrug resistance.
Nosocomial outbreaks are common due to its ability to survive in healthcare environments.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · MUCOCUTANEOUS CANDIDA INFECTIONS

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Vulvovaginal Candidiasis (VVC)
items.content: VVC is diagnosed through culture and microscopy of vaginal secretions, with treatment options including topical or oral azoles, as well as newer agents like ibrexafungerp.
keyPoints: Diagnosis involves culture and microscopy of vaginal secretions collected via swabs.
Treatment may include topical or oral azoles.

Newer treatments include ibrexafungerp, which inhibits β-glucan, and 1723 otesconazole, a long-acting tetrazole not suitable for patients of reproductive potential due to potential fetal ocular toxicity.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · MUCOCUTANEOUS CANDIDA INFECTIONS

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Transmission of Dermatophyte Infections
items.content: Dermatophytes, including Trichophyton, Microsporum, and Epidermophyton, are molds causing common skin infections globally. These fungi live in keratinized structures and can be transmitted through direct contact or fomites like hairbrushes and wet floors. Some species also spread from animals to humans.
keyPoints: ~1 billion people affected worldwide
Transmission via person-to-person, fomites, and animals
IL-17 responses restrict infections; more common in males

items.title: Recognizing Clinical Manifestations of Dermatophyte Infections
items.content: Dermatophyte infections, often called tinea or ringworm, present with characteristic lesions. Tinea capitis affects 3- to 7-year-olds with scaly patches and alopecia; tinea corporis shows annular lesions; tinea cruris is seen in men with erythematous, pruritic rashes; tinea pedis causes peeling and maceration between toes.
keyPoints: Tinea capitis: 3- to 7-year-olds
Tinea corporis: annular lesions
Tinea cruris: erythematous, pruritic rashes in men

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Dermatophyte Infections
items.content: Dermatophyte infections are often diagnosed clinically but can be confirmed with potassium hydroxide microscopy or cultures. Topical azoles or terbinafine treat most skin infections, while oral itraconazole or terbinafine is used for extensive cases. Onychomycosis requires systemic treatment due to poor response to topical agents.
keyPoints: Diagnosis: clinical appearance, KOH microscopy
Topical therapy: azoles, terbinafine
Onychomycosis: systemic itraconazole or terbinafine

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Transmission of Sporotrichosis
items.content: Sporotrichosis is primarily caused by Sporothrix schenckii complex, with S. schenckii and S. brasiliensis being the most common pathogens in humans. The disease is associated with outdoor activities involving contact with sphagnum moss, decaying vegetation, or soil. Transmission can occur through traumatic inoculation from infected animals like cats, which have been notably implicated in an outbreak in Brazil.
keyPoints: Sporothrix schenckii complex includes six organisms; S. schenckii and S. brasiliensis are most common in humans.
S. globosa is increasingly significant in Asia.
Transmission often occurs through contact with decaying vegetation or infected animals, especially cats.
Outbreaks have been linked to activities like landscaping, gardening, and tree farming.

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Sporotrichosis
items.content: Sporotrichosis is diagnosed by culturing S. schenckii from a cutaneous lesion biopsy on Sabouraud's agar at room temperature, with histopathologic examination revealing granulomatous reactions and yeast forms. Itraconazole is the preferred treatment for cutaneous and lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis, while saturated solution of potassium iodide (SSKI) remains an alternative due to its low cost.
keyPoints: Diagnosis involves culturing on Sabouraud's agar; histopathology shows granulomatous reactions with yeast forms.
Itraconazole is the drug of choice for cutaneous and lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis.
Fluconazole is less effective, voriconazole ineffective, posaconazole used in some cases.
SSKI is an alternative treatment option due to low cost.

items.question: Which areas of the body are primarily inhabited by Malassezia species as part of the indigenous human microbiota?
items.explanation: Malassezia species are part of the indigenous human microbiota found in areas rich in sebaceous glands such as the back, chest, scalp, and face.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · Superficial Fungal Infections | MALASSEZIA INFECTIONS

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of Malassezia species infection that presents with flat round scaly patches?
items.explanation: Tinea versicolor, also called pityriasis versicolor, presents as flat round scaly patches of hypo- or hyperpigmented skin.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · Superficial Fungal Infections | MALASSEZIA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which treatment is NOT typically used for Malassezia infections?
items.explanation: Nystatin ointment is not typically used for Malassezia infections; selenium sulfide, ketoconazole, and terbinafine are common treatments.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · Superficial Fungal Infections | MALASSEZIA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which Candida species is primarily responsible for mucocutaneous candidiasis such as oral thrush, esophageal candidiasis, and vulvovaginal candidiasis?
items.explanation: Candida albicans is the most common species responsible for mucocutaneous candidiasis.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · MUCOCUTANEOUS CANDIDA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which of the following risk factors is NOT associated with vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC)?
items.explanation: AIDS is a risk factor for oral thrush, not specifically for VVC.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · MUCOCUTANEOUS CANDIDA INFECTIONS

items.question: What type of cells mediate control against Candida at mucocutaneous barrier sites?
items.explanation: IL-17-producing lymphoid cells, such as T_H17 cells, γδ T cells, and innate lymphoid cells, mediate control against Candida.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · MUCOCUTANEOUS CANDIDA INFECTIONS

items.question: Which genera are responsible for causing dermatophyte infections?
items.explanation: The passage states that the genera Trichophyton, Microsporum, and Epidermophyton are responsible for dermatophyte infections.

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of tinea capitis in children?
options: Erythematous and pustular rash without satellite lesions
Scaliness and maceration between the toes
items.explanation: Tinea capitis in children presents as well-demarcated scaly patches where hair shafts are broken off, leading to alopecia.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common site for tinea infections?
items.explanation: Tinea infections affect keratinized structures like skin and nails, not internal organs such as lungs.

items.question: Which treatment is generally the first-line systemic agent for dermatophyte infections?
items.explanation: The passage indicates that terbinafine interacts with fewer drugs than itraconazole and is generally the first-line systemic agent for dermatophyte infections.

items.question: Which species of Sporothrix are most commonly associated with human infections?
items.explanation: S. schenckii and S. brasiliensis are the species that cause most human infections.

items.question: Which animal is primarily responsible for transmitting sporotrichosis in a large outbreak in Brazil?
items.explanation: A large ongoing outbreak of sporotrichosis in Brazil caused by S. brasiliensis has been traced to cats.

items.question: What is the primary mode of transmission for Sporothrix species?
items.explanation: Sporotrichosis is primarily a localized infection of skin and subcutaneous tissues that follows traumatic conidial inoculation.

items.statement: Malassezia species can cause invasive infections in individuals with healthy immune systems.
items.explanation: Invasive infection by Malassezia species almost always occurs in premature neonates receiving parenteral lipid nutrition, not typically in those with healthy immune systems.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · Superficial Fungal Infections | MALASSEZIA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Seborrheic dermatitis can be severe in patients with AIDS.
items.explanation: Seborrheic dermatitis can indeed be more severe in immunocompromised individuals, such as those with AIDS.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · Superficial Fungal Infections | MALASSEZIA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Candida auris is known for its susceptibility to multiple antifungal treatments.
items.explanation: Candida auris is an emerging multidrug-resistant species.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · MUCOCUTANEOUS CANDIDA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Biopsy of affected oral or esophageal tissue in candidiasis shows only yeast forms superficial to epithelial tissue.
items.explanation: Biopsy shows yeasts, pseudohyphae, and hyphae invading into the epithelium; observing only yeast forms indicates colonization.
items.reference: Superficial Fungal Infections · MUCOCUTANEOUS CANDIDA INFECTIONS

items.statement: Dermatophytes are part of the normal skin microbiota.
items.explanation: The passage states that dermatophytes are not components of the normal skin microbiota.

items.statement: Tinea pedis can lead to lower-extremity cellulitis.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that tinea pedis has been implicated in lower-extremity cellulitis due to streptococci and staphylococci gaining entrance through fissures.

items.statement: Pulmonary sporotrichosis occurs almost exclusively in persons with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
items.explanation: Pulmonary sporotrichosis occurs almost exclusively in persons with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease following fungal inhalation.

items.statement: Fluconazole is the drug of choice for cutaneous and lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis.
items.explanation: Itraconazole is the drug of choice for cutaneous and lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis.

items.assertion: Tinea cruris is seen almost exclusively in men.
items.reason: The rash of tinea cruris has a discrete scaly border and is usually pruritic.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why tinea cruris is seen almost exclusively in men.

items.assertion: Onychomycosis can be treated effectively with topical therapy.
items.reason: Efinaconazole topical solution has been beneficial in several trials for onychomycosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but systemic treatment is generally preferred as topical therapy often does not respond well.
